channel.title: Home on Artist Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Artist Portfolio

item.title: Untitled I
item.description:
This piece explores the tension between structure and organic form. Painted over the course of several weeks, the layered oils create a depth that shifts depending on the light in the room.

Process

The work began as a series of quick gestural sketches, which were then translated onto a large-format canvas. Each layer was allowed to dry fully before the next was applied, building up a rich surface texture.

Dimensions

120 × 80 cm

item.title: Untitled II
item.description:
A study in texture and layering, this piece explores the interplay between translucent washes and opaque mark-making.

Process

The work began as an experiment with fluid acrylics on heavyweight watercolor paper. Multiple layers were built up over several sessions, allowing each to dry fully before the next application.

Key techniques used:

  • Wet-on-wet washes for the background gradients
  • Dry brush strokes for the textural foreground elements
  • Palette knife work for the thicker impasto sections

Inspiration

This piece was inspired by the weathered surfaces found in old Lisbon — peeling paint, crumbling plaster, and the way time leaves its mark on the built environment.

item.title: Untitled III
item.description:
A series of digital illustrations exploring the intersection of organic forms and geometric precision.

Concept

This piece began as a study of natural patterns — the branching of trees, the spiral of shells — reinterpreted through a strict digital grid. The tension between the two systems became the subject itself.

Process

Working in layers, I started with scanned ink drawings and progressively transformed them through algorithmic distortion. Each pass stripped away some of the hand-drawn quality while introducing new symmetries.

item.title: Untitled IV
item.description:
A mixed media piece combining found materials with paint and ink. The layering process creates depth and texture that invites closer inspection.

Materials

This piece incorporates:

  • Acrylic paint
  • India ink
  • Newspaper clippings
  • Handmade paper

Process

The work began as a series of ink washes on handmade paper, onto which I collaged fragments of newspaper text. The final layers of acrylic paint partially obscure the text, creating a dialogue between the visible and the hidden.

item.title: Untitled V
item.description:
A photographic study exploring light, shadow, and the quiet drama of everyday spaces.

Concept

This work began as a series of walks through the city at dusk, capturing the moments where natural light gives way to artificial glow. The resulting images sit in a liminal space — neither fully day nor night.

Process

Shot on medium format film over the course of several weeks. Each frame was chosen for its sense of stillness and the way light pooled in unexpected corners.

item.title: Untitled VI
item.description:
A study in transparency and flow, this watercolor piece explores how pigment settles into the texture of cold-pressed paper.

About This Piece

Working wet-on-wet, I allowed the colors to bleed and merge organically, guiding the composition with minimal intervention. The result captures a sense of spontaneity that is difficult to achieve with more controlled media.

The palette was intentionally restrained — just three pigments mixed directly on the paper surface.

item.title: Horizon Study
item.description:
A wide-format painting exploring the dissolving boundary between sky and land. The horizontal emphasis forces the eye to travel slowly across the surface, encountering subtle shifts in temperature and saturation.

Concept

The horizon line has always been a subject of fascination — it is the one line in nature that exists purely as a perceptual phenomenon. You can never reach it, never touch it. This piece attempts to paint that impossibility.

item.title: Panorama I
item.description:
A wide-format painting that demands to be read left to right, like a sentence. The panoramic composition stretches the viewer’s gaze across a continuous field of muted color, resisting the urge to settle on a single focal point.

Concept

This piece is part of an ongoing investigation into peripheral vision and the way we absorb landscape not as a framed moment but as a slow lateral scan. The proportions — nearly 3:1 — were chosen to push against the conventions of easel painting.

item.title: Wide Field
item.description:
A horizontal composition exploring the relationship between vast open space and the edges that contain it. The wide format was chosen deliberately to echo the experience of peripheral vision.

Concept

This piece grew out of a fascination with how we perceive landscape — not as a framed rectangle, but as an expansive, borderless field. The elongated canvas format forces the viewer’s eye to travel laterally, mimicking the slow scan of a horizon line.

item.title: Landscape Series
item.description:
A panoramic study of the Alentejo plains at midday, when the heat flattens everything into bands of color. The extreme horizontal format mirrors the experience of standing in that landscape — the horizon line stretching beyond peripheral vision.

Concept

This piece belongs to an ongoing series examining how wide-format compositions alter the viewer’s relationship to depicted space. By pushing the canvas to a 2.5:1 ratio, the eye is forced to travel laterally rather than settling on a central focal point.
